message.content:
we need to write documentation and adjust readme, adjust CLAUDE.md, and claude code review workflow file
and put warrning and info regarding 3 phases releases in case of breacking changes in DB schema
@CLAUDE.md @README.md @CONTRIBUTING.md @docs/ @.github/workflows/claude-code-review.yml 

context.. we use ktool that handles deployemnt and cron job and migrations runs
ktool first deploys new code and launches pods with new code alongside while old pods with new code are still alive (it happens in multiregion us-east-1 and 2 with 3-10 pods in each region) and only after new code launched it runs migration...
it means we need to make each release properly to avoid downtime and data loss and errors.
We came up with Idea
3-phase migration strategy
Phase 1 — Expand (deploy with old code)

Add new columns/tables as nullable, keep old ones
Old code ignores new stuff, keeps working

Phase 2 — Migrate (deploy new code)

New code writes to BOTH old + new columns
Backfill script runs
Old pods still work if rollback needed

Phase 3 — Contract (next release)

Drop old columns/code paths
Only after 100% traffic on new code

if you check codebase all our hht endpoint only read from DB, but cronjobs and event sync can write to the DB.

@src/modules/cli/commands/sync-source-styles.command.ts 
@src/modules/db/


create detalied instruction in readme for devs, then broad documentation with examples and diagrams 
then check in claude-code review that we do not have breacking changes and instructions in claude.md or other files that will help claude to build our feature right..

if you need more answers - ask me or use internet to get best practices for zero downtime deployment strategies

file.content:
projectName: 'recommendations-service'
tier: 2
team: 'recommendations'
namespace: 'ecommerce'

iam_role:
  enabled: true

envs:
  PORT: 8080
  GLOBAL_PREFIX: '/api/v1'
  SERVER_NAME: 'reco-ms'
  IS_COMPACT_LOGS: true
  IS_JSON_LIKE_LOGS: true
  EXECUTION_TIME_THRESHOLD_MS: 1000

  prod:
    NODE_ENV: 'production'
    AWS_REGION: 'us-east-1'
    ALG_INDEX_NAME: 'reco_styles_prod'
    GRACEFUL_SHUTDOWN_DELAY: 4000
    MMS_IMAGES_LAMBDA_HOST: 'https://mms-images.out.customink.com'
  staging: &envs_staging
    NODE_ENV: 'staging'
    AWS_REGION: 'us-east-1'
    ALG_INDEX_NAME: 'reco_styles_staging'
    GRACEFUL_SHUTDOWN_DELAY: 4000
    MMS_IMAGES_LAMBDA_HOST: 'https://mms-images.staging.customink.com'
  dev:
    <<: *envs_staging

secrets:
  parameterStore:
    - SHARED/NEWRELIC_KEY
    - REDIS_URL
    - REDIS_READ_URL
    - ALG_APP_ID
    - ALG_API_KEY
    - ROLLBAR_ACCESS_TOKEN
    - OPENAI_API_KEY
    - ARTILLERY_KEY
    - RELATED_PRODUCTS_CACHE_TTL
    - CI_BASE_URL
    - MMS_INTERNAL_BASE_URL
    - DATABASE_URL
    - DATABASE_URL_READ_REPLICA
    - ALG_FBT_INDEX_NAME
    - AWS_SQS_QUEUE_URL
    - REDSHIFT_READ_URL
    - CURATED_ADDONS_CACHE_TTL
    - BIGQUERY_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_KEY

objects:
  - type: web
    sslSidecar: true
    startup_probe:
      endpoint: /is_it_up
    liveness_probe:
      endpoint: /is_it_up
    readiness_probe:
      endpoint: /is_it_working
    prod:
      ingress:
        external:
          enabled: true
          hosts:
            - recommendations-service.out.customink.com
        internal:
          enabled: true
          hosts:
            - recommendations-service.in.customink.com
      extraAnnotations:
        live.stormforge.io/auto-deploy: 'Enabled'
      resources:
        requests:
          cpu: 256m
          memory: 1Gi
        limits:
          cpu: 6
          memory: 6Gi
      autoscaling:
        minReplicas: 4
        maxReplicas: 10
        cpu:
          threshold: 90
        behavior:
          scaleDown:
            coolDown: 3000
    staging:
      ingress:
        external:
          enabled: true
          hosts:
            - recommendations-service.out.staging.customink.com
        internal:
          enabled: true
          hosts:
            - recommendations-service.in.staging.customink.com
      extraAnnotations:
        live.stormforge.io/auto-deploy: 'Enabled'
      resources:
        requests:
          cpu: 128m
          memory: 1Gi
        limits:
          cpu: 3
          memory: 3Gi
      autoscaling:
        minReplicas: 2
        maxReplicas: 3
        cpu:
          threshold: 90
        behavior:
          scaleDown:
            coolDown: 3000
    dev:
      ingress:
        internal:
          enabled: true
      resources:
        requests:
          cpu: 128m
          memory: 1Gi
        limits:
          cpu: 2
          memory: 2Gi
        replicas: 1
    command:
      - npm
      - run
      - start:prod
  - type: cron_job
    name: sync-source-styles
    schedule: 0 9 * * *
    resources:
      requests:
        cpu: 4
        memory: 8Gi
      limits:
        cpu: 8
        memory: 12Gi
      extraAnnotations:
        live.stormforge.io/auto-deploy: 'Disabled'
    command:
      - ./bin/cron-sync-source-styles
  - type: cron_job
    name: populate-trending-items
    schedule: 0 5 * * 6
    resources:
      requests:
        cpu: 12
        memory: 6Gi
      limits:
        cpu: 12
        memory: 10Gi
      extraAnnotations:
        live.stormforge.io/auto-deploy: 'Enabled'
    command:
      - ./bin/cron-trending-items
  - type: cron_job
    name: populate-style-copurchases
    schedule: 0 6 * * 0
    resources:
      requests:
        cpu: 4
        memory: 4Gi
      limits:
        cpu: 8
        memory: 8Gi
      extraAnnotations:
        live.stormforge.io/auto-deploy: 'Enabled'
    command:
      - ./bin/cron-populate-style-copurchases
  - type: db_migration
    command:
      - npm
      - run
      - cli:prod
      - migrate

file.content:
# Database Migrations

This document describes the database migration tools and commands available in the recommendations service.

## Migration Commands

The service provides both npm scripts and CLI commands for managing database migrations:

### NPM Scripts

```bash
# Run all pending migrations
npm run migrate

# Run one migration up
npm run migrate:up

# Run one migration down
npm run migrate:down

# Rollback the last batch of migrations
npm run migrate:rollback

# Create a new migration file
npm run migrate:make your_migration_name
```

### CLI Commands

The service also provides CLI commands through the nest-commander framework:

```bash
# Using development CLI
npm run cli:dev migrate-up
npm run cli:dev migrate-down
npm run cli:dev migrate-rollback
npm run cli:dev migrate

# Using production CLI
npm run cli:prod migrate-up
npm run cli:prod migrate-down
npm run cli:prod migrate-rollback
npm run cli:prod migrate
```

## Migration Files

Migration files are located in `/src/migrations/` and use TypeScript with the `.ts` extension.

When creating new migrations:
- Use descriptive names that explain the change
- Follow the existing naming convention: `YYYYMMDDHHMMSS_descriptive_name.ts`
- Ensure both `up` and `down` methods are implemented

## Configuration

Migrations are configured through:
- `src/knexfile.ts` - Main knex configuration
- `src/knexfile.prod.ts` - Production-specific configuration
- Environment variables for database connection settings

## Best Practices

1. **Always test migrations**: Test both up and down migrations in development
2. **Backup before production**: Always backup the database before running migrations in production
3. **Incremental changes**: Keep migrations small and focused on single changes
4. **Rollback capability**: Ensure every migration has a proper rollback strategy
5. **Data migrations**: Be careful with data transformations and consider data loss scenarios

## Database Replication

The service uses MySQL replication with separate read/write connections. Migrations only run on the write database and replicate to read replicas automatically.

## Troubleshooting

- If a migration fails, check the database logs and migration lock table
- Use `migrate:rollback` to undo the last batch if needed
- For production issues, consider running migrations manually with proper monitoring

# MySQL Master-Slave Replication Setup

This setup creates a local MySQL master-slave replication environment that mimics your AWS Aurora production setup with separate read and write endpoints.

## Architecture

```
┌─────────────────┐    ┌─────────────────┐
│   MySQL Master │────▶│   MySQL Slave   │
│   (Write DB)    │    │   (Read DB)     │
│   Port: 3336    │    │   Port: 3337    │
└─────────────────┘    └─────────────────┘
        │                       │
        ▼                       ▼
  Write Operations      Read Operations
```

## Configuration Details

### Master Database (Write Endpoint)

- **Container**: `mysql-master`
- **Port**: `3336` (internal), `3336` (host)
- **Connection**: `mysql://app_user:app_password@mysql-master:3336/recommendations`
- **Role**: Handles all write operations
- **Features**: Binary logging enabled, GTID enabled

### Slave Database (Read Endpoint)

- **Container**: `mysql-slave`

Base directory for this skill: /Users/4thwithme/Documents/repo/recommendations-service/.claude/skills/diagram-skill

# Diagram Skill

Create technical diagrams using ASCII art for documentation and visualization.

## When to Use

Trigger when:
- Visualizing feature flows or business logic
- Documenting API interactions
- Showing database relationships
- Illustrating class hierarchies
- Mapping system architecture
- Explaining request/response cycles
- Documenting data flows

## Diagram Types

### Sequence Diagrams (ASCII Art)

Show interactions between components over time using boxes, lines, and arrows.

**Pattern:**

```
┌─────────┐           ┌────────────┐         ┌─────────┐         ┌──────────┐
│ Client  │           │ Controller │         │ Service │         │ Database │
└────┬────┘           └─────┬──────┘         └────┬────┘         └────┬─────┘
     │                      │                     │                   │
     │ POST /api/endpoint   │                     │                   │
     │─────────────────────>│                     │                   │
     │                      │                     │                   │
     │                      │  processData(dto)   │                   │
     │                      │────────────────────>│                   │
     │                      │                     │                   │
     │                      │                     │  INSERT INTO      │
     │                      │                     │──────────────────>│
     │                      │                     │                   │
     │                      │                     │      Result       │
     │                      │                     │<──────────────────│
     │                      │                     │                   │
     │                      │   ProcessedData     │                   │
     │                      │<────────────────────│                   │
     │                      │                     │                   │
     │    201 Created       │                     │                   │
     │<─────────────────────│                     │                   │
     │                      │                     │                   │
```

**Elements:**
- `┌─┐ └─┘` - Component boxes
- `─────>` - Request arrow (solid)
- `<─────` - Response arrow (dashed can use `- - ->`)
- `│` - Vertical lifeline
- Text labels for messages

**Example: NestJS API Flow**

```
┌─────────┐   ┌──────────────────────┐   ┌──────────────────────┐   ┌──────────────┐   ┌────────────┐   ┌─────────────────┐
│ Client  │   │ RelatedProductsCtrl  │   │ RelatedProductsSvc   │   │ CacheService │   │ Knex(Read) │   │ AlgoliaService  │
└────┬────┘   └──────────┬───────────┘   └──────────┬───────────┘   └──────┬───────┘   └─────┬──────┘   └────────┬────────┘
     │                   │                           │                      │                 │                   │
     │ GET /related-     │                           │                      │                 │                   │
     │ products/:styleId │                           │                      │                 │                   │
     │──────────────────>│                           │                      │                 │                   │
     │                   │                           │                      │                 │                   │
     │                   │ getRelatedProducts()      │                      │                 │                   │
     │                   │──────────────────────────>│                      │                 │                   │
     │                   │                           │                      │                 │                   │
     │                   │                           │  get(namespace, key) │                 │                   │
     │                   │                           │─────────────────────>│                 │                   │
     │                   │                           │                      │                 │                   │
     │                   │                           │    null (cache miss) │                 │                   │
     │                   │                           │<─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─│                 │                   │
     │                   │                           │                      │                 │                   │
     │                   │                           │  SELECT * FROM styles WHERE...         │                   │
     │                   │                           │────────────────────────────────────────>│                   │
     │                   │                           │                      │                 │                   │
     │                   │                           │           StyleData  │                 │                   │
     │                   │                           │<─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─│                   │
     │                   │                           │                      │                 │                   │
     │                   │                           │  searchSimilar(styleData)              │                   │
     │                   │                           │────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────>│
     │                   │                           │                      │                 │                   │
     │                   │                           │                      │                 │  RelatedProducts[]│
     │                   │                           │<─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─│
     │                   │                           │                      │                 │                   │
     │                   │                           │  set(namespace, key, data, ttl)        │                   │
     │                   │                           │─────────────────────>│                 │                   │
     │                   │                           │                      │                 │                   │
     │                   │                           │          OK          │                 │                   │
     │                   │                           │<─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─│                 │                   │
     │                   │                           │                      │                 │                   │
     │                   │   RelatedProductsDto      │                      │                 │                   │
     │                   │<─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ │                      │                 │                   │
     │                   │                           │                      │                 │                   │
     │  200 OK + JSON    │                           │                      │                 │                   │
     │<─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ │                           │                      │                 │                   │
     │                   │                           │                      │                 │                   │
```

### Class Diagrams (ASCII Art)

Show class structure and relationships.

**Pattern:**

```
┌──────────────────────────┐
│    BaseEntityDto         │
├──────────────────────────┤
│ + _id: string            │
│ + created_at: Date       │
│ + updated_at: Date       │
│ + deleted_at: Date|null  │
└──────────┬───────────────┘
           │
           │ extends
           ├─────────────────────┐
           │                     │
┌──────────▼───────────┐  ┌──────▼────────────────┐
│  CreateEntityDto     │  │  UpdateEntityDto      │
├──────────────────────┤  ├───────────────────────┤
│ + name: string       │  │ + name?: string       │
│ + entity_id: number  │  │ + entity_id?: number  │
│ + validate(): bool   │  └───────────────────────┘
└──────────────────────┘

┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│         EntityModel                 │
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│ - knexRead: Knex                    │
│ - knexWrite: Knex                   │
│ - logger: Logger                    │
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│ + create(data): Promise<IEntity>    │
│ + findById(id): Promise<IEntity>    │
│ + update(id, data): Promise<IEntity>│
│ + softDelete(id): Promise<void>     │
└─────────────────────────────────────┘
           │
           │ uses
           ├──────────────┬──────────────┐
           │              │              │
           ▼              ▼              ▼
   CreateEntityDto  UpdateEntityDto  BaseEntityDto
```

**Elements:**
- `┌─┐ └─┘ ├ ┤ ┬ ┴ ┼` - Box drawing
- `│` - Vertical separator
- `─` - Horizontal separator
- `▼ ▲` - Arrows for relationships
- `+` public, `-` private, `#` protected

### Entity-Relationship Diagrams (ASCII Art)

Show database table relationships.

**Pattern:**

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│            styles                   │
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│ PK  _id (UUID)                      │
│ UK  style_id (BIGINT)               │
│ FK  primary_category_id (BIGINT)    │
│ FK  root_category_id (BIGINT)       │
│     purchases_count (BIGINT)        │
│     created_at (TIMESTAMP)          │
│     updated_at (TIMESTAMP)          │
│     deleted_at (TIMESTAMP)          │
└─────────────┬───────────────────────┘
              │ 1
              │
              │ has
              │
              │ *
    ┌─────────┴──────────┬──────────────────┐
    │                    │                  │
    ▼                    ▼                  ▼
┌─────────────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────────┐
│  style_colors   │  │  styles_hash     │  │ condensed_features  │
├─────────────────┤  ├──────────────────┤  ├─────────────────────┤
│ PK _id          │  │ PK _id           │  │ PK _id              │
│ FK style_id     │  │ FK style_id      │  │ FK style_id         │
│    color_id     │  │ UK hash          │  │    condensed_json   │
│    color_rgb    │  │    algorithm     │  │    created_at       │
│    color_name   │  │    created_at    │  └─────────────────────┘
│    created_at   │  └──────────────────┘
└─────────────────┘

Legend:
  PK = Primary Key
  FK = Foreign Key
  UK = Unique Key
  1  = One
  *  = Many
```

### Flowchart (ASCII Art)

Show decision flows and processes.

**Pattern:**

```
                    ┌────────────────────┐
                    │  Start: API Request│
                    └─────────┬──────────┘
                              │
                              ▼
                    ┌────────────────────┐
                    │  Valid Input?      │
                    └─────┬──────────┬───┘
                          │          │
                     Yes  │          │  No
                          │          │
                          ▼          ▼
                ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐
                │ Check Cache  │  │ Return 400 Error │
                └──────┬───────┘  └────────┬─────────┘
                       │                   │
                       │                   │
                ┌──────▼─────────┐         │
                │  Cache Hit?    │         │
                └────┬────────┬──┘         │
                     │        │            │
                 Yes │        │ No         │
                     │        │            │
                     ▼        ▼            │
          ┌─────────────┐  ┌────────────┐ │
          │Return Cached│  │Query DB    │ │
          └──────┬──────┘  └─────┬──────┘ │
                 │                │        │
                 │                ▼        │
                 │         ┌────────────┐  │
                 │         │Process Data│  │
                 │         └─────┬──────┘  │
                 │               │         │
                 │               ▼         │
                 │         ┌────────────┐  │
                 │         │Save Cache  │  │
                 │         └─────┬──────┘  │
                 │               │         │
                 └───────┬───────┘         │
                         │                 │
                         ▼                 │
                   ┌───────────┐           │
                   │Return Data│           │
                   └─────┬─────┘           │
                         │                 │
                         └─────────┬───────┘
                                   │
                                   ▼
                            ┌────────────┐
                            │    End     │
                            └────────────┘
```

**Elements:**
- `┌─┐ └─┘` - Process boxes
- `◇` - Decision diamonds (or use box format)
- `─────>` - Flow arrows
- Labels on arrows

## Best Practices

### Clear Component Names

Use full, descriptive names in boxes:
```
┌────────────────────────┐
│ RelatedProductsController │
└────────────────────────┘
```

### Show Activation/Deactivation

Use indentation or annotations:
```
Controller
  │ [ACTIVE]
  │──> Service
  │      [ACTIVE]
  │      └──> Database
  │            [ACTIVE]
  │            Result
  │      <────
  │      [INACTIVE]
  │ <────
  │ [INACTIVE]
```

### Add Notes

Use text annotations:
```
     │  get(key)        │
     │─────────────────>│
     │                  │  // Checks Redis cache
     │     null         │  // with 1-hour TTL
     │<─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─│
```

### Error Paths

Show alternative flows clearly:
```
    ┌──────────────┐
    │ Validate DTO │
    └──────┬───────┘
           │
    ┌──────▼─────────┐
    │  Valid?        │
    └──┬────────────┬┘
       │ Yes        │ No
       │            │
       ▼            ▼
   [Success]    [400 Error]
    Path         Path
```

### Parallel Operations

Show side-by-side:
```
Service
  │
  ├─────────────────┬─────────────────┬─────────────────┐
  │                 │                 │                 │
  ▼                 ▼                 ▼                 ▼
┌────────┐     ┌────────┐     ┌────────┐     ┌────────┐
│ Fetch  │     │ Fetch  │     │ Fetch  │     │ Fetch  │
│ Users  │     │ Orders │     │Products│     │ Stats  │
└───┬────┘     └───┬────┘     └───┬────┘     └───┬────┘
    │              │              │              │
    └──────────────┴──────────────┴──────────────┘
                   │
                   ▼
            Combine Results
```

## Common NestJS Patterns

### CRUD Operation with Guards

```
┌─────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌───────┐  ┌──────────┐
│ Client  │  │AuthGuard │  │ Controller │  │ValidationPipe  │  │ Service │  │ Model │  │ Knex(W)  │
└────┬────┘  └────┬─────┘  └─────┬──────┘  └───────┬────────┘  └────┬────┘  └───┬───┘  └────┬─────┘
     │            │               │                 │                 │           │           │
     │ POST /api  │               │                 │                 │           │           │
     │───────────>│               │                 │                 │           │           │
     │            │               │                 │                 │           │           │
     │            │ Verify JWT    │                 │                 │           │           │
     │            │───────────────>│                 │                 │           │           │
     │            │               │                 │                 │           │           │
     │            │    Authorized │                 │                 │           │           │
     │            │<───────────────│                 │                 │           │           │
     │            │               │                 │                 │           │           │
     │            │               │  Validate DTO   │                 │           │           │
     │            │               │────────────────>│                 │           │           │
     │            │               │                 │                 │           │           │
     │            │               │    Valid DTO    │                 │           │           │
     │            │               │<────────────────│                 │           │           │
     │            │               │                 │                 │           │           │
     │            │               │      create(dto)│                 │           │           │
     │            │               │────────────────────────────────────>           │           │
     │            │               │                 │                 │           │           │
     │            │               │                 │                 │create(data)           │
     │            │               │                 │                 │──────────>│           │
     │            │               │                 │                 │           │           │
     │            │               │                 │                 │           │INSERT INTO│
     │            │               │                 │                 │           │──────────>│
     │            │               │                 │                 │           │           │
     │            │               │                 │                 │           │  Result   │
     │            │               │                 │                 │           │<──────────│
     │            │               │                 │                 │           │           │
     │            │               │                 │                 │   Entity  │           │
     │            │               │                 │                 │<──────────│           │
     │            │               │                 │                 │           │           │
     │            │               │                 │      EntityDto  │           │           │
     │            │               │<────────────────────────────────────           │           │
     │            │               │                 │                 │           │           │
     │ 201 Created│               │                 │                 │           │           │
     │<───────────────────────────│                 │                 │           │           │
     │            │               │                 │                 │           │           │
```

### Database Read/Write Pattern

```
API Request
    │
    ▼
┌───────────────┐
│ Determine Op  │
└───────┬───────┘
        │
        │
    ┌───▼────┐
    │  Type? │
    └───┬────┘
        │
  ┌─────┴─────┐
  │           │
Read          Write
  │           │
  ▼           ▼
┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐
│Knex Read │ │Knex Write│
│Connection│ │Connection│
└────┬─────┘ └────┬─────┘
     │            │
     │            ▼
     │      ┌────────────┐
     │      │Transaction │
     │      └────┬───────┘
     │           │
     └─────┬─────┘
           │
           ▼
      ┌─────────┐
      │Response │
      └─────────┘
```

### Caching Strategy

```
┌─────────┐   ┌─────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐
│ Client  │   │ Service │   │  Cache   │   │ Database │
└────┬────┘   └────┬────┘   └────┬─────┘   └────┬─────┘
     │             │              │              │
     │ getData(id) │              │              │
     │────────────>│              │              │
     │             │              │              │
     │             │ get(key)     │              │
     │             │─────────────>│              │
     │             │              │              │
     │             │   Hit/Miss?  │              │
     │             │              │              │
     │             ├──────────────┤              │
     │             │              │              │
     │         If Hit:            │              │
     │             │ Data         │              │
     │             │<─────────────│              │
     │             │              │              │
     │      Data   │              │              │
     │<────────────│              │              │
     │             │              │              │
     │         If Miss:           │              │
     │             │              │              │
     │             │        Query database       │
     │             │───────────────────────────> │
     │             │              │              │
     │             │              │    Data      │
     │             │<─────────────────────────── │
     │             │              │              │
     │             │ set(key,data)│              │
     │             │─────────────>│              │
     │             │              │              │
     │             │      OK      │              │
     │             │<─────────────│              │
     │             │              │              │
     │      Data   │              │              │
     │<────────────│              │              │
     │             │              │              │
```

## Output Format

Output diagrams as code blocks:

```
[ASCII art diagram here]
```

No special rendering needed - pure text displays everywhere.

## Success Checklist

Good ASCII diagram:
- ✅ Clear component boxes with full names
- ✅ Logical flow (top-to-bottom or left-to-right)
- ✅ Aligned lines and arrows
- ✅ Labels on all arrows/connections
- ✅ Consistent spacing and formatting
- ✅ Error paths shown (if applicable)
- ✅ Notes for complex operations
- ✅ Readable in any text editor/viewer
- ✅ Uses box-drawing characters properly

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/4thwithme/Documents/repo/recommendations-service/.claude/skills/documentation-skill

# Documentation Skill

Create and maintain comprehensive technical documentation in `/docs/` with code examples and ASCII art diagrams.

## When to Use

Trigger when:
- User asks to document features or logic
- New features need documentation
- Existing docs need updates
- Explaining system architecture
- Describing API endpoints
- Documenting database schemas
- Tool/utility documentation needed
- Business logic needs explanation

## Documentation Location

All documentation lives in:
```
docs/
├── features/           # Feature documentation
├── architecture/       # System architecture
├── api/               # API documentation
├── database/          # Database schemas
├── tools/             # CLI tools & utilities
└── guides/            # How-to guides
```

## Documentation Structure

### Standard Template

Every documentation file should include:

1. **Title & Overview**
2. **Use Cases**
3. **Architecture** (with diagrams)
4. **Data Flow** (with ASCII sequence diagrams for features/business logic)
5. **Code Examples**
6. **API Reference** (if applicable)
7. **Configuration** (if applicable)
8. **Testing**
9. **Troubleshooting**

## Workflow

### 1. Understand What to Document

Analyze:
- Feature/module code
- Related services
- Database models
- API endpoints
- Configuration

### 2. Determine Documentation Type

**Feature Documentation:**
- Location: `docs/features/{feature-name}.md`
- **MUST include ASCII sequence diagram** showing flow
- Includes use cases, architecture, examples

**Architecture Documentation:**
- Location: `docs/architecture/{component}.md`
- System design, patterns, decisions
- Class diagrams, ER diagrams

**API Documentation:**
- Location: `docs/api/{endpoint}.md`
- Endpoint details, request/response examples
- Sequence diagrams for complex flows

**Database Documentation:**
- Location: `docs/database/{table}.md`
- Schema, relationships, indexes
- ER diagrams

### 3. Use diagram-skill for Diagrams

For **every feature or business logic** documentation:

**REQUIRED: ASCII Sequence Diagram**
- Shows complete request/response flow
- Includes all components (Controller, Service, Model, DB, Cache, 3rd party APIs)
- Shows error paths
- Uses diagram-skill to generate ASCII art

**Optional: Additional Diagrams**
- Class diagrams for complex relationships
- ER diagrams for database docs
- Flowcharts for decision trees

### 4. Write Documentation

Follow template pattern below.

### 5. Save to Appropriate Directory

Create file in correct location under `/docs/`.

## Performance Considerations

- **Caching**: All reads cached for 1 hour
- **Database**: Uses read replica for SELECT queries
- **Batch Operations**: Processes in batches of 100
- **Indexes**: Optimized for name lookups

## Troubleshooting

### Common Issues

**Issue: Cache miss on every request**
- **Cause**: Cache key not constructed correctly
- **Solution**: Verify namespace and key format

**Issue: Slow queries**
- **Cause**: Missing index on frequently queried column
- **Solution**: Add index in migration

**Issue: Validation errors**
- **Cause**: DTO validation rules not met
- **Solution**: Check class-validator decorators

## Related Documentation

- [Database Schema](../database/features.md)
- [Caching Strategy](../architecture/caching.md)
- [API Guidelines](../guides/api-guidelines.md)

# Zero-Downtime Database Migrations

## Why This Matters

Our deployment pipeline (managed by **ktool**) runs **new code before migrations**. Every release follows this order:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                         ktool Deployment Order                                  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                 │
│  Step 1  │  Old pods still running (old code + old schema)                      │
│          │                                                                      │
│  Step 2  │  New pods START  ← new code, but old schema (migration hasn't run)   │
│          │                                                                      │
│  Step 3  │  New pods pass health checks                                         │
│          │                                                                      │
│  Step 4  │  Migration runs  ← DB schema changes while BOTH pod versions alive   │
│          │                                                                      │
│  Step 5  │  Old pods drain  ← old code runs against NEW schema briefly          │
│                                                                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Two constraints must hold simultaneously:**

- New code must work with the **old schema** (steps 2–3, pre-migration)
- Old code must work with the **new schema** (step 5, post-migration)

This applies to both regions (us-east-1, us-east-2) with 3–10 pods each.

### Who Writes to the DB?

HTTP controllers in `src/modules/rest/` are **read-only**. All writes come from:

| Writer | Location |
|--------|----------|
| Cron jobs | `src/modules/cli/commands/` |
| SQS event handlers | `src/modules/*/` services |
| Stored procedures | Triggered by cron jobs |

This narrows the blast radius for write-path changes but does not eliminate the risk.

---

## The 3-Phase Release Strategy

Any schema change that is not backward-compatible **must be spread across 3 separate releases**.

### Full Deployment Timeline

```
Phase 1 Release (Expand):
  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                           │
  │  [Old pods: v0]          ────────────────────────── drain ───────────────>│
  │  [New pods: v1 expand]   ──── healthy ────────────────────────────────── ►│
  │                                    │                                      │
  │                           [db_migration: v1]                              │
  │                           ADD COLUMN new_col NULL                         │
  │                                                                           │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Phase 2 Release (Migrate - dual-write):
  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                           │
  │  [Pods: v1 expand]       ────────────────── drain ───────────────────────>│
  │  [New pods: v2 migrate]  ──── healthy ────────────────────────────────── ►│
  │                                    │                                      │
  │                           [db_migration: v2]                              │
  │                           UPDATE table SET new_col = old_col              │
  │                           WHERE new_col IS NULL                           │
  │                                                                           │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Phase 3 Release (Contract - cleanup):
  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                           │
  │  [Pods: v2 migrate]      ────────────────── drain ───────────────────────>│
  │  [New pods: v3 contract] ──── healthy ────────────────────────────────── ►│
  │                                    │                                      │
  │                           [db_migration: v3]                              │
  │                           DROP COLUMN old_col                             │
  │                                                                           │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

### Phase 1 — Expand

**Goal**: Widen the schema so old and new code can coexist.

```
What to do in the migration:
  ✅ Add new columns as NULLABLE (no NOT NULL constraint yet)
  ✅ Add new tables
  ✅ Add new indexes

What NOT to do in code yet:
  ❌ Do not read from new columns in application code
  ❌ Do not write to new columns in application code
  → Ship the migration only, no feature code changes
```

**Why it is safe:**

- Old code ignores unknown columns
- New pods come up with old schema → migration adds nullable columns → no reads/writes break
- Old pods after migration see new nullable columns → silently ignored

**Migration template:**

```typescript
export async function up(knex: Knex): Promise<void> {
  await knex.transaction(async (trx) => {
    await trx.schema.alterTable(TABLE_NAME, (table) => {
      table.string('new_col', 255).nullable();          // safe: nullable
      table.integer('new_count').nullable().defaultTo(0); // safe: has default
    });
  });
}

export async function down(knex: Knex): Promise<void> {
  await knex.transaction(async (trx) => {
    await trx.schema.alterTable(TABLE_NAME, (table) => {
      table.dropColumn('new_count');
      table.dropColumn('new_col');
    });
  });
}
```

---

### Phase 2 — Migrate

**Goal**: Deploy code that uses new columns. Handle both pre- and post-migration state.

**Critical rule**: New code deploys **before** its own migration runs. Between pod startup and migration completion, new code runs against old schema — code must be defensive.

```
Phase 2 data flow for a cron job writer:

┌─────────────────┐      ┌────────────────────┐      ┌───────────────────────┐
│  Cron Job Pod   │      │   MySQL Write DB   │      │   MySQL Read Replica  │
│  (v2 new code)  │      │  (running on both  │      │  (catches up from     │
│                 │      │   old+new schema)  │      │   write DB)           │
└────────┬────────┘      └─────────┬──────────┘      └───────────┬───────────┘
         │                         │                              │
         │  [pods start, migration │                              │
         │   hasn't run yet]       │                              │
         │                         │                              │
         │  UPDATE table           │                              │
         │  SET old_col = value,   │                              │
         │      new_col = value    │ ◄── dual-write               │
         │ ─────────────────────►  │                              │
         │                         │                              │
         │  [migration runs]       │                              │
         │  BACKFILL new_col       │                              │
         │  WHERE new_col IS NULL  │                              │
         │  ─────────────────────► │                              │
         │                         │                              │
         │  [old pods draining]    │                              │
         │  old code reads          │                              │
         │  old_col (still exists) │◄─────────────────────────── │
         │                         │                              │
         │  [only new pods alive]  │                              │
         │  new code reads          │                              │
         │  new_col (backfilled)   │◄─────────────────────────── │
         │                         │                              │
```

**Code pattern:**

```typescript
// Writing (cron job / event handler)
await knex(TABLE_NAME).update({
  old_col: value,     // keep writing old column — old pods may still roll back
  new_col: value,     // write new column
});

// Reading with null guard (new code before migration runs)
const record = await knexRead(TABLE_NAME).first();
const value = record.new_col ?? record.old_col;
```

**Migration for backfill:**

```typescript
export async function up(knex: Knex): Promise<void> {
  await knex(TABLE_NAME)
    .whereNull('new_col')
    .update({ new_col: knex.ref('old_col') });
}

export async function down(knex: Knex): Promise<void> {
  await knex(TABLE_NAME)
    .whereNull('old_col')
    .update({ old_col: knex.ref('new_col') });
}
```

---

### Phase 3 — Contract

**Goal**: Remove old columns and dead code. **Only ship after Phase 2 has been running in production for at least one full cron job cycle.**

```
Checklist before shipping Phase 3:
  ✅ All cron jobs have run at least once with v2 code
  ✅ new_col has 0 NULL values in production (verify before merging)
  ✅ No rollback is pending for Phase 2
  ✅ Monitoring shows no errors related to old_col reads
```

**Migration:**

```typescript
export async function up(knex: Knex): Promise<void> {
  await knex.transaction(async (trx) => {
    await trx.schema.alterTable(TABLE_NAME, (table) => {
      table.dropColumn('old_col');
    });
  });
}

export async function down(knex: Knex): Promise<void> {
  // WARNING: data is gone — restoring schema only
  await knex.transaction(async (trx) => {
    await trx.schema.alterTable(TABLE_NAME, (table) => {
      table.string('old_col', 255).nullable();
    });
  });
}
```

**Code cleanup:**

```typescript
// Remove dual-write
await knex(TABLE_NAME).update({ new_col: value }); // no more old_col

// Remove null guard
const value = record.new_col; // always populated now
```

---

## Safe vs Breaking Changes (Quick Reference)

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────┬────────────────────────┐
│ Change                                             │ Safe in 1 release?     │ Strategy               │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┤
│ Add nullable column                                │ ✅ Yes                 │ Single release         │
│ Add column with default                            │ ✅ Yes                 │ Single release         │
│ Create new table                                   │ ✅ Yes                 │ Single release         │
│ Add index (small table)                            │ ✅ Yes                 │ Single release         │
│ Add index (large table, e.g. styles_mv)            │ ⚠️  Use INPLACE        │ ALGORITHM=INPLACE      │
│ Widen column (VARCHAR 100→255)                     │ ✅ Yes                 │ Single release         │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┤
│ Drop column                                        │ ❌ No                  │ 3-phase                │
│ Rename column                                      │ ❌ No                  │ 3-phase                │
│ Change type incompatibly (JSON→VARCHAR)            │ ❌ No                  │ 3-phase                │
│ Add NOT NULL without default                       │ ❌ No                  │ 3-phase                │
│ Drop table                                         │ ❌ No                  │ 3-phase                │
│ Add UNIQUE on existing data                        │ ❌ No                  │ 3-phase (dedup first)  │
│ Narrow column (VARCHAR 255→10)                     │ ❌ No                  │ 3-phase                │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────┴────────────────────────┘
```

---

## Practical Examples

### Example A: Add a Nullable Column (single release)

Safe. New code and old code both ignore or handle a new nullable column gracefully.

```typescript
// Migration
export async function up(knex: Knex): Promise<void> {
  await knex.schema.alterTable(TABLE_NAME_STYLES_MV, (table) => {
    table.string('supplier_name', 255).nullable();
  });
}
```

---

### Example B: Rename a Column (3-phase)

**Context**: Rename `score` (INT) → `relevance_score` (FLOAT) on `style_copurchases`.

**Phase 1 — Add new column:**

```typescript
export async function up(knex: Knex): Promise<void> {
  await knex.schema.alterTable(TABLE_NAME_STYLE_COPURCHASES, (table) => {
    table.float('relevance_score').nullable();
  });
}
```

**Phase 2 — Dual-write + backfill:**

```typescript
// Migration: backfill
export async function up(knex: Knex): Promise<void> {
  await knex(TABLE_NAME_STYLE_COPURCHASES)
    .whereNull('relevance_score')
    .update({ relevance_score: knex.raw('CAST(score AS FLOAT)') });
}

// Cron job code: dual-write
await knex(TABLE_NAME_STYLE_COPURCHASES).update({
  score: Math.round(value),          // old column (old pods may read it)
  relevance_score: value,            // new column
});

// Read with fallback
const score = record.relevance_score ?? record.score;
```

**Phase 3 — Drop old column:**

```typescript
// Migration: drop old
export async function up(knex: Knex): Promise<void> {
  await knex.schema.alterTable(TABLE_NAME_STYLE_COPURCHASES, (table) => {
    table.dropColumn('score');
  });
}

// Code: remove dual-write and fallback
await knex(TABLE_NAME_STYLE_COPURCHASES).update({ relevance_score: value });
const score = record.relevance_score;
```

---

### Example C: Add NOT NULL Column (3-phase)

**Context**: Add `priority` INTEGER NOT NULL to `promotional_products`.

**Phase 1 — Add as nullable:**

```typescript
await trx.schema.alterTable(TABLE_NAME_PROMOTIONAL_PRODUCTS, (table) => {
  table.integer('priority').nullable();
});
```

**Phase 2 — Backfill, then write from code:**

```typescript
// Migration: backfill default
await knex(TABLE_NAME_PROMOTIONAL_PRODUCTS)
  .whereNull('priority')
  .update({ priority: 0 });

// Writer code: always provide value
await knex(TABLE_NAME_PROMOTIONAL_PRODUCTS).insert({
  ...data,
  priority: data.priority ?? 0,
});
```

**Phase 3 — Add NOT NULL constraint:**

```typescript
await trx.schema.alterTable(TABLE_NAME_PROMOTIONAL_PRODUCTS, (table) => {
  table.integer('priority').notNullable().defaultTo(0).alter();
});
```

---

### Example D: Large Table Batched Backfill

For tables with millions of rows (`styles_mv`, `style_copurchases`), never do a
single UPDATE. Use batches:

```typescript
export async function up(knex: Knex): Promise<void> {
  const BATCH_SIZE = 1000;

  while (true) {
    const count = await knex(TABLE_NAME)
      .whereNull('new_col')
      .limit(BATCH_SIZE)
      .update({ new_col: knex.ref('old_col') });

    if (count === 0) break;

    // brief pause to avoid overwhelming the write DB
    await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, 50));
  }
}
```

For large-table indexes, use MySQL online DDL:

```typescript
await knex.raw(
  `ALTER TABLE \`${TABLE_NAME}\`
   ADD INDEX \`idx_new_col_deleted_at\` (\`new_col\`, \`deleted_at\`)
   ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=NONE`
);
```

---

## Identifying All Writers Before Any Migration

Before changing any column, find every write path:

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              Where to look for DB writers                  │
├──────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┤
│ Writer type              │ Location                        │
├──────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
│ Cron jobs                │ src/modules/cli/commands/       │
│ SQS event handlers       │ src/modules/*/services          │
│ Stored procedures        │ src/migrations/*_procedure.ts   │
│ Seed scripts             │ src/seeds/                      │
├──────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
│ HTTP controllers         │ src/modules/rest/  → READ ONLY  │
└──────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

## PR Labels and Checklist

### PR Title Convention for 3-Phase Changes

```
RECO-1234 [Phase 1]: Add new_col to style_copurchases
RECO-1234 [Phase 2]: Dual-write new_col, backfill existing rows
RECO-1234 [Phase 3]: Drop old_col from style_copurchases
```

Link all 3 tickets to each other in Jira.

### PR Checklist (schema changes)

- [ ] Identified all write paths for affected table(s)
- [ ] Confirmed safe in single release OR split into 3 phases
- [ ] Phase label added to PR title: `[Phase 1]`, `[Phase 2]`, `[Phase 3]`
- [ ] New code handles `null` values defensively (Phase 2 only)
- [ ] Dual-write implemented for all writers (Phase 2 only)
- [ ] Large-table operations use `INPLACE` or batched approach
- [ ] `down()` migration is implemented and tested locally
- [ ] Phase 1 and Phase 2 tickets linked (for Phase 3 PRs)

---

## Rollback

Every migration must have a working `down()` function.

```
Rollback commands:
  npm run migrate:rollback   — roll back last batch
  npm run migrate:down       — roll back one migration
```

> **Warning**: Rolling back a Phase 3 drop restores the column schema but not the data.
> Never reach Phase 3 without high confidence that Phase 2 is stable.

---

## Related Documentation

- [Database Migrations](../database-migrations.md) — migration commands and configuration
- [MySQL Replication Setup](../devcontainer-mysql-replica.md) — local read/write replica setup
- [Cron Jobs](../cron-jobs.md) — cron job configuration and scheduling
- [CLI Module](../../src/modules/cli/CLAUDE.md) — CLI command development guide

toolUseResult.content:
# Zero-Downtime Database Migrations

## Why This Matters

Our deployment pipeline (managed by **ktool**) runs **new code before migrations**. Every release follows this order:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                         ktool Deployment Order                                  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                 │
│  Step 1  │  Old pods still running (old code + old schema)                      │
│          │                                                                      │
│  Step 2  │  New pods START  ← new code, but old schema (migration hasn't run)   │
│          │                                                                      │
│  Step 3  │  New pods pass health checks                                         │
│          │                                                                      │
│  Step 4  │  Migration runs  ← DB schema changes while BOTH pod versions alive   │
│          │                                                                      │
│  Step 5  │  Old pods drain  ← old code runs against NEW schema briefly          │
│                                                                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Two constraints must hold simultaneously:**

- New code must work with the **old schema** (steps 2–3, pre-migration)
- Old code must work with the **new schema** (step 5, post-migration)

This applies to both regions (us-east-1, us-east-2) with 3–10 pods each.

### Who Writes to the DB?

HTTP controllers in `src/modules/rest/` are **read-only**. All writes come from:

| Writer | Location |
|--------|----------|
| Cron jobs | `src/modules/cli/commands/` |
| SQS event handlers | `src/modules/*/` services |
| Stored procedures | Triggered by cron jobs |

This narrows the blast radius for write-path changes but does not eliminate the risk.

---

## The 3-Phase Release Strategy

Any schema change that is not backward-compatible **must be spread across 3 separate releases**.

### Full Deployment Timeline

```
Phase 1 Release (Expand):
  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                           │
  │  [Old pods: v0]          ────────────────────────── drain ───────────────>│
  │  [New pods: v1 expand]   ──── healthy ────────────────────────────────── ►│
  │                                    │                                      │
  │                           [db_migration: v1]                              │
  │                           ADD COLUMN new_col NULL                         │
  │                                                                           │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Phase 2 Release (Migrate - dual-write):
  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                           │
  │  [Pods: v1 expand]       ────────────────── drain ───────────────────────>│
  │  [New pods: v2 migrate]  ──── healthy ────────────────────────────────── ►│
  │                                    │                                      │
  │                           [db_migration: v2]                              │
  │                           UPDATE table SET new_col = old_col              │
  │                           WHERE new_col IS NULL                           │
  │                                                                           │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Phase 3 Release (Contract - cleanup):
  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                           │
  │  [Pods: v2 migrate]      ────────────────── drain ───────────────────────>│
  │  [New pods: v3 contract] ──── healthy ────────────────────────────────── ►│
  │                                    │                                      │
  │                           [db_migration: v3]                              │
  │                           DROP COLUMN old_col                             │
  │                                                                           │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

### Phase 1 — Expand

**Goal**: Widen the schema so old and new code can coexist.

```
What to do in the migration:
  ✅ Add new columns as NULLABLE (no NOT NULL constraint yet)
  ✅ Add new tables
  ✅ Add new indexes

What NOT to do in code yet:
  ❌ Do not read from new columns in application code
  ❌ Do not write to new columns in application code
  → Ship the migration only, no feature code changes
```

**Why it is safe:**

- Old code ignores unknown columns
- New pods come up with old schema → migration adds nullable columns → no reads/writes break
- Old pods after migration see new nullable columns → silently ignored

**Migration template:**

```typescript
export async function up(knex: Knex): Promise<void> {
  await knex.transaction(async (trx) => {
    await trx.schema.alterTable(TABLE_NAME, (table) => {
      table.string('new_col', 255).nullable();          // safe: nullable
      table.integer('new_count').nullable().defaultTo(0); // safe: has default
    });
  });
}

export async function down(knex: Knex): Promise<void> {
  await knex.transaction(async (trx) => {
    await trx.schema.alterTable(TABLE_NAME, (table) => {
      table.dropColumn('new_count');
      table.dropColumn('new_col');
    });
  });
}
```

---

### Phase 2 — Migrate

**Goal**: Deploy code that uses new columns. Handle both pre- and post-migration state.

**Critical rule**: New code deploys **before** its own migration runs. Between pod startup and migration completion, new code runs against old schema — code must be defensive.

```
Phase 2 data flow for a cron job writer:

┌─────────────────┐      ┌────────────────────┐      ┌───────────────────────┐
│  Cron Job Pod   │      │   MySQL Write DB   │      │   MySQL Read Replica  │
│  (v2 new code)  │      │  (running on both  │      │  (catches up from     │
│                 │      │   old+new schema)  │      │   write DB)           │
└────────┬────────┘      └─────────┬──────────┘      └───────────┬───────────┘
         │                         │                              │
         │  [pods start, migration │                              │
         │   hasn't run yet]       │                              │
         │                         │                              │
         │  UPDATE table           │                              │
         │  SET old_col = value,   │                              │
         │      new_col = value    │ ◄── dual-write               │
         │ ─────────────────────►  │                              │
         │                         │                              │
         │  [migration runs]       │                              │
         │  BACKFILL new_col       │                              │
         │  WHERE new_col IS NULL  │                              │
         │  ─────────────────────► │                              │
         │                         │                              │
         │  [old pods draining]    │                              │
         │  old code reads          │                              │
         │  old_col (still exists) │◄─────────────────────────── │
         │                         │                              │
         │  [only new pods alive]  │                              │
         │  new code reads          │                              │
         │  new_col (backfilled)   │◄─────────────────────────── │
         │                         │                              │
```

**Code pattern:**

```typescript
// Writing (cron job / event handler)
await knex(TABLE_NAME).update({
  old_col: value,     // keep writing old column — old pods may still roll back
  new_col: value,     // write new column
});

// Reading with null guard (new code before migration runs)
const record = await knexRead(TABLE_NAME).first();
const value = record.new_col ?? record.old_col;
```

**Migration for backfill:**

```typescript
export async function up(knex: Knex): Promise<void> {
  await knex(TABLE_NAME)
    .whereNull('new_col')
    .update({ new_col: knex.ref('old_col') });
}

export async function down(knex: Knex): Promise<void> {
  await knex(TABLE_NAME)
    .whereNull('old_col')
    .update({ old_col: knex.ref('new_col') });
}
```

---

### Phase 3 — Contract

**Goal**: Remove old columns and dead code. **Only ship after Phase 2 has been running in production for at least one full cron job cycle.**

```
Checklist before shipping Phase 3:
  ✅ All cron jobs have run at least once with v2 code
  ✅ new_col has 0 NULL values in production (verify before merging)
  ✅ No rollback is pending for Phase 2
  ✅ Monitoring shows no errors related to old_col reads
```

**Migration:**

```typescript
export async function up(knex: Knex): Promise<void> {
  await knex.transaction(async (trx) => {
    await trx.schema.alterTable(TABLE_NAME, (table) => {
      table.dropColumn('old_col');
    });
  });
}

export async function down(knex: Knex): Promise<void> {
  // WARNING: data is gone — restoring schema only
  await knex.transaction(async (trx) => {
    await trx.schema.alterTable(TABLE_NAME, (table) => {
      table.string('old_col', 255).nullable();
    });
  });
}
```

**Code cleanup:**

```typescript
// Remove dual-write
await knex(TABLE_NAME).update({ new_col: value }); // no more old_col

// Remove null guard
const value = record.new_col; // always populated now
```

---

## Safe vs Breaking Changes (Quick Reference)

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────┬────────────────────────┐
│ Change                                             │ Safe in 1 release?     │ Strategy               │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┤
│ Add nullable column                                │ ✅ Yes                 │ Single release         │
│ Add column with default                            │ ✅ Yes                 │ Single release         │
│ Create new table                                   │ ✅ Yes                 │ Single release         │
│ Add index (small table)                            │ ✅ Yes                 │ Single release         │
│ Add index (large table, e.g. styles_mv)            │ ⚠️  Use INPLACE        │ ALGORITHM=INPLACE      │
│ Widen column (VARCHAR 100→255)                     │ ✅ Yes                 │ Single release         │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┤
│ Drop column                                        │ ❌ No                  │ 3-phase                │
│ Rename column                                      │ ❌ No                  │ 3-phase                │
│ Change type incompatibly (JSON→VARCHAR)            │ ❌ No                  │ 3-phase                │
│ Add NOT NULL without default                       │ ❌ No                  │ 3-phase                │
│ Drop table                                         │ ❌ No                  │ 3-phase                │
│ Add UNIQUE on existing data                        │ ❌ No                  │ 3-phase (dedup first)  │
│ Narrow column (VARCHAR 255→10)                     │ ❌ No                  │ 3-phase                │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────┴────────────────────────┘
```

---

## Practical Examples

### Example A: Add a Nullable Column (single release)

Safe. New code and old code both ignore or handle a new nullable column gracefully.

```typescript

export async function up(knex: Knex): Promise<void> {
  await knex.schema.alterTable(TABLE_NAME_STYLES_MV, (table) => {
    table.string('supplier_name', 255).nullable();
  });
}
```

---

### Example B: Rename a Column (3-phase)

**Context**: Rename `score` (INT) → `relevance_score` (FLOAT) on `style_copurchases`.

**Phase 1 — Add new column:**

```typescript
export async function up(knex: Knex): Promise<void> {
  await knex.schema.alterTable(TABLE_NAME_STYLE_COPURCHASES, (table) => {
    table.float('relevance_score').nullable();
  });
}
```

**Phase 2 — Dual-write + backfill:**

```typescript

export async function up(knex: Knex): Promise<void> {
  await knex(TABLE_NAME_STYLE_COPURCHASES)
    .whereNull('relevance_score')
    .update({ relevance_score: knex.raw('CAST(score AS FLOAT)') });
}

// Cron job code: dual-write
await knex(TABLE_NAME_STYLE_COPURCHASES).update({
  score: Math.round(value),          // old column (old pods may read it)

// Read with fallback

```

**Phase 3 — Drop old column:**

```typescript

export async function up(knex: Knex): Promise<void> {

    table.dropColumn('score');

}

// Code: remove dual-write and fallback
await knex(TABLE_NAME_STYLE_COPURCHASES).update({ relevance_score: value });

```

---

### Example C: Add NOT NULL Column (3-phase)

**Context**: Add `priority` INTEGER NOT NULL to `promotional_products`.

**Phase 1 — Add as nullable:**

```typescript
await trx.schema.alterTable(TABLE_NAME_PROMOTIONAL_PRODUCTS, (table) => {
  table.integer('priority').nullable();
});
```

**Phase 2 — Backfill, then write from code:**

```typescript
// Migration: backfill default
await knex(TABLE_NAME_PROMOTIONAL_PRODUCTS)
  .whereNull('priority')
  .update({ priority: 0 });

// Writer code: always provide value
await knex(TABLE_NAME_PROMOTIONAL_PRODUCTS).insert({
  ...data,
  priority: data.priority ?? 0,
});
```

**Phase 3 — Add NOT NULL constraint:**

```typescript
await trx.schema.alterTable(TABLE_NAME_PROMOTIONAL_PRODUCTS, (table) => {
  table.integer('priority').notNullable().defaultTo(0).alter();
});
```

---

### Example D: Large Table Batched Backfill

For tables with millions of rows (`styles_mv`, `style_copurchases`), never do a
single UPDATE. Use batches:

```typescript
export async function up(knex: Knex): Promise<void> {
  const BATCH_SIZE = 1000;

  while (true) {
    const count = await knex(TABLE_NAME)
      .whereNull('new_col')
      .limit(BATCH_SIZE)
      .update({ new_col: knex.ref('old_col') });

    if (count === 0) break;

    // brief pause to avoid overwhelming the write DB
    await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, 50));
  }
}
```

For large-table indexes, use MySQL online DDL:

```typescript
await knex.raw(
  `ALTER TABLE \`${TABLE_NAME}\`
   ADD INDEX \`idx_new_col_deleted_at\` (\`new_col\`, \`deleted_at\`)
   ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=NONE`
);
```

---

## Identifying All Writers Before Any Migration

Before changing any column, find every write path:

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              Where to look for DB writers                  │
├──────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┤
│ Writer type              │ Location                        │
├──────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
│ Cron jobs                │ src/modules/cli/commands/       │
│ SQS event handlers       │ src/modules/*/services          │
│ Stored procedures        │ src/migrations/*_procedure.ts   │
│ Seed scripts             │ src/seeds/                      │
├──────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
│ HTTP controllers         │ src/modules/rest/  → READ ONLY  │
└──────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

## PR Labels and Checklist

### PR Title Convention for 3-Phase Changes

```
RECO-1234 [Phase 1]: Add new_col to style_copurchases
RECO-1234 [Phase 2]: Dual-write new_col, backfill existing rows
RECO-1234 [Phase 3]: Drop old_col from style_copurchases
```

Link all 3 tickets to each other in Jira.

### PR Checklist (schema changes)

- [ ] Identified all write paths for affected table(s)
- [ ] Confirmed safe in single release OR split into 3 phases
- [ ] Phase label added to PR title: `[Phase 1]`, `[Phase 2]`, `[Phase 3]`
- [ ] New code handles `null` values defensively (Phase 2 only)
- [ ] Dual-write implemented for all writers (Phase 2 only)
- [ ] Large-table operations use `INPLACE` or batched approach
- [ ] `down()` migration is implemented and tested locally
- [ ] Phase 1 and Phase 2 tickets linked (for Phase 3 PRs)

---

## Rollback

Every migration must have a working `down()` function.

```
Rollback commands:
  npm run migrate:rollback   — roll back last batch
  npm run migrate:down       — roll back one migration
```

> **Warning**: Rolling back a Phase 3 drop restores the column schema but not the data.
> Never reach Phase 3 without high confidence that Phase 2 is stable.

---

## Related Documentation

- [Database Migrations](../database-migrations.md) — migration commands and configuration
- [MySQL Replication Setup](../devcontainer-mysql-replica.md) — local read/write replica setup
- [Cron Jobs](../cron-jobs.md) — cron job configuration and scheduling
- [CLI Module](../../src/modules/cli/CLAUDE.md) — CLI command development guide

input.content:
# Database Migrations

## Overview

This document covers migration commands and tooling. For schema change safety requirements,
see **[Zero-Downtime Migrations](./database/zero-downtime-migrations.md)** — required reading
before writing any migration that modifies existing columns or tables.

---

## Migration Commands

### NPM Scripts

```bash
npm run migrate             # Run all pending migrations
npm run migrate:up          # Run one migration up
npm run migrate:down        # Run one migration down
npm run migrate:rollback    # Rollback the last batch
npm run migrate:make name   # Create a new migration file
```

### CLI Commands

```bash
npm run cli:dev migrate
npm run cli:dev migrate-up
npm run cli:dev migrate-down
npm run cli:dev migrate-rollback

npm run cli:prod migrate    # runs automatically via ktool db_migration
```

---

## Migration Files

Located in `src/migrations/`. Naming convention:

```
YYYYMMDDHHMMSS_descriptive_name.ts
```

Every migration file must export `up()` and `down()`:

```typescript
import { Logger } from '@nestjs/common';
import { TABLE_NAME_EXAMPLE } from '@constants/db.constant';
import type { Knex } from 'knex';

const logger = new Logger('YYYYMMDDHHMMSS_migration_name.ts');

export async function up(knex: Knex): Promise<void> {
  logger.log('Running migration: ...');
  await knex.transaction(async (trx) => {
    // migration logic
  });
}

export async function down(knex: Knex): Promise<void> {
  logger.log('Reverting migration: ...');
  await knex.transaction(async (trx) => {
    // rollback logic
  });
}
```

---

## Deployment Order (ktool)

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 1. New code pods start (old schema, migration not run yet)  │
│ 2. New pods pass health checks                              │
│ 3. ktool runs: type: db_migration → npm run cli:prod migrate│
│ 4. Old pods drain                                           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**New code must work with the old schema.** See zero-downtime migration guide.

---

## Best Practices

1. **Always use transactions** — wrap DDL and DML in `knex.transaction()`
2. **Always implement `down()`** — tested locally before merging
3. **Idempotent creates** — use `hasTable()` checks before `createTable()`
4. **Batched backfills** — never a single UPDATE on large tables; process in 1000-row batches
5. **Online DDL for large tables** — use `ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=NONE` for index operations
6. **3-phase for breaking changes** — dropping/renaming/type-changing columns requires 3 releases

---

## Configuration

- `src/knexfile.ts` — development/test configuration
- `src/knexfile.prod.ts` — production configuration
- Database connection via `DATABASE_URL` and `DATABASE_URL_READ_REPLICA` secrets

---

## Troubleshooting

- **Migration lock**: if a migration was interrupted, the knex lock table may be stuck — check and clear manually
- **Rollback**: `migrate:rollback` undoes the last batch; `migrate:down` undoes one step
- **Replica lag**: migrations run on the write DB; read replicas catch up automatically

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Database Migrations

This document describes the database migration tools and commands available in the recommendations service.

## Migration Commands

The service provides both npm scripts and CLI commands for managing database migrations:

### NPM Scripts

```bash
# Run all pending migrations
npm run migrate

# Run one migration up
npm run migrate:up

# Run one migration down
npm run migrate:down

# Rollback the last batch of migrations
npm run migrate:rollback

# Create a new migration file
npm run migrate:make your_migration_name
```

### CLI Commands

The service also provides CLI commands through the nest-commander framework:

```bash
# Using development CLI
npm run cli:dev migrate-up
npm run cli:dev migrate-down
npm run cli:dev migrate-rollback
npm run cli:dev migrate

# Using production CLI
npm run cli:prod migrate-up
npm run cli:prod migrate-down
npm run cli:prod migrate-rollback
npm run cli:prod migrate
```

## Migration Files

Migration files are located in `/src/migrations/` and use TypeScript with the `.ts` extension.

When creating new migrations:
- Use descriptive names that explain the change
- Follow the existing naming convention: `YYYYMMDDHHMMSS_descriptive_name.ts`
- Ensure both `up` and `down` methods are implemented

## Configuration

Migrations are configured through:
- `src/knexfile.ts` - Main knex configuration
- `src/knexfile.prod.ts` - Production-specific configuration
- Environment variables for database connection settings

## Best Practices

1. **Always test migrations**: Test both up and down migrations in development
2. **Backup before production**: Always backup the database before running migrations in production
3. **Incremental changes**: Keep migrations small and focused on single changes
4. **Rollback capability**: Ensure every migration has a proper rollback strategy
5. **Data migrations**: Be careful with data transformations and consider data loss scenarios

## Database Replication

The service uses MySQL replication with separate read/write connections. Migrations only run on the write database and replicate to read replicas automatically.

## Troubleshooting

- If a migration fails, check the database logs and migration lock table
- Use `migrate:rollback` to undo the last batch if needed
- For production issues, consider running migrations manually with proper monitoring

### Database Migrations (CRITICAL — Read Before Writing Any Migration)

Full guide: **[docs/database/zero-downtime-migrations.md](./docs/database/zero-downtime-migrations.md)**

**ktool deployment order**: new code pods start → health checks pass → migration runs → old pods drain.
This means new code runs against the old schema until the migration completes.

#### Breaking Changes Require 3 Releases

Any of the following **MUST** be spread across 3 separate releases:

| Forbidden in single release | Use instead |
|-----------------------------|-------------|
| `table.dropColumn(...)` | Phase 1: stop writing → Phase 3: drop |
| `table.renameColumn(...)` | Phase 1: add new col → Phase 2: dual-write → Phase 3: drop old |
| Column type change (incompatible) | Phase 1: add new col → Phase 2: dual-write → Phase 3: drop old |
| `notNullable()` on existing column without default | Phase 1: nullable → Phase 2: backfill → Phase 3: add constraint |
| `dropTable(...)` | Phase 1: remove all code refs → Phase 3: drop |

#### Safe in a Single Release

- Adding a nullable column
- Adding a column with a default value
- Creating a new table
- Adding an index (use `ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=NONE` for large tables)
- Widening a column (e.g. VARCHAR 100→255)

#### Phase 2 Code Rules (dual-write + null guard)

When deploying Phase 2, new code **must** handle the old schema (migration hasn't run yet):

```typescript
// Writing — dual-write keeps old pods safe during drain
await knex(TABLE_NAME).update({
  old_col: value,   // old pods still read this
  new_col: value,   // new column
});

// Reading — new_col may be null before migration backfills it
const value = record.new_col ?? record.old_col;
```

#### PR Title Convention for 3-Phase Changes

```
RECO-1234 [Phase 1]: Add new_col to table_name
RECO-1234 [Phase 2]: Dual-write new_col, backfill rows
RECO-1234 [Phase 3]: Drop old_col from table_name
```

#### Writers That Must Be Updated

HTTP controllers in `src/modules/rest/` are **read-only**. All DB writers:
- Cron jobs: `src/modules/cli/commands/`
- Event handlers: `src/modules/*/` services
- Stored procedures: `src/migrations/*_procedure.ts`

### Git Workflow

- **Branches**: `RECO-XXXX-suffix`
- **Commits**: `RECO-XXXX: Description in imperative mood`
- **PRs**: `RECO-XXXX: Short description`, requires Code Owner approval, squash merge only
